event: Paleo-Indian hunter-gatherers inhabit Patagonia, later leaving the painted handprints of the Cueva de las Manos.
Agricultural societies emerge in the northwest, cultivating maize and developing pottery in the Andean valleys.
The Aguada culture flourishes in the northwest, producing sophisticated metalwork and ceramics.
The Inca Empire expands southward, incorporating much of present-day northwestern Argentina into Collasuyu.
Spanish explorer Juan Diaz de Solis reaches the Rio de la Plata, the first European to arrive in the region.
Pedro de Mendoza founds the first settlement at Buenos Aires, soon abandoned after indigenous resistance.
Jeronimo Luis de Cabrera founds the city of Cordoba, advancing Spanish colonization of the interior.
Juan de Garay re-establishes Buenos Aires as a permanent settlement on the Rio de la Plata.
Spain creates the Viceroyalty of the Rio de la Plata with Buenos Aires as its capital.
Local militias repel British invasions of Buenos Aires, boosting colonial confidence and self-government.
The May Revolution overthrows the Spanish viceroy and installs the first local governing junta.
The Congress of Tucuman declares the independence of the United Provinces of the Rio de la Plata from Spain.
Jose de San Martin leads the Army of the Andes across the mountains to help liberate Chile and Peru.
Civil wars erupt between Unitarian centralists and Federalists over the structure of the new nation.
Juan Manuel de Rosas becomes governor of Buenos Aires, beginning a long period of federalist dictatorship.
Rosas is defeated at the Battle of Caseros, opening the way for a national constitution.
The Argentine Constitution is adopted, establishing a federal republic.
Bartolome Mitre becomes the first president of a unified Argentina after Buenos Aires rejoins the confederation.
Argentina enters the War of the Triple Alliance against Paraguay alongside Brazil and Uruguay.
The Conquest of the Desert under General Roca subdues indigenous peoples and opens Patagonia to settlement.
Buenos Aires is federalized as the national capital, ending decades of regional conflict.
Argentina celebrates its centennial as one of the world's wealthiest nations amid mass European immigration.
The Saenz Pena Law introduces universal, secret, and compulsory male suffrage.
Hipolito Yrigoyen of the Radical Civic Union wins the first election under the new suffrage law.
A military coup ousts Yrigoyen, beginning the Infamous Decade of electoral fraud and conservative rule.
Argentina joins the United Nations as a founding member.
Juan Domingo Peron is elected president, launching the Peronist movement built on labor and social reform.
Women gain the right to vote, championed by Eva Peron.
Eva Peron dies of cancer, becoming an enduring icon of Argentine popular politics.
The Revolucion Libertadora military coup overthrows Peron and forces him into exile.
A military coup installs General Ongania, beginning the authoritarian Argentine Revolution.
Peron returns from exile and is again elected president after years of political turmoil.
Peron dies in office and is succeeded by his wife Isabel, amid escalating political violence.
A military junta seizes power and launches the Dirty War, killing or disappearing tens of thousands.
Argentina hosts and wins the FIFA World Cup amid the military dictatorship.
Argentina invades the Falkland Islands but is defeated by Britain in the Falklands War.
Raul Alfonsin is elected president, restoring democracy after the dictatorship's collapse.
The Trial of the Juntas convicts former military leaders for crimes against humanity.
Argentina wins the FIFA World Cup in Mexico, led by Diego Maradona.
Carlos Menem becomes president amid hyperinflation and begins sweeping free-market reforms.
The Convertibility Plan pegs the peso to the US dollar, temporarily taming hyperinflation.
A bombing of the AMIA Jewish community center in Buenos Aires kills 85 people.
A severe economic crisis triggers riots, a sovereign default, and the resignation of President de la Rua.
Nestor Kirchner becomes president and oversees economic recovery and human rights prosecutions.
Cristina Fernandez de Kirchner is elected as the country's first elected female president.
Argentina legalizes same-sex marriage, the first country in Latin America to do so.
Argentine Cardinal Jorge Bergoglio is elected Pope Francis, the first pope from the Americas.
Mauricio Macri wins the presidency, ending twelve years of Kirchnerist rule.
Argentina legalizes abortion, a landmark for reproductive rights in Latin America.
Argentina wins the FIFA World Cup in Qatar, captained by Lionel Messi.
Libertarian economist Javier Milei is elected president amid soaring inflation and economic crisis.
